title: How Solar Magnetism Makes Sunspots Rise and Fall

lesson.learn: Sunspots are cooler, darker regions on the Sun's visible surface that form where magnetic fields become especially strong. The number of sunspots rises and falls in a roughly 11-year pattern called the Schwabe cycle. That cycle is part of a much larger solar dynamo process driven by the Sun's rotation, convection, and magnetic field reversal. The pattern matters because it tracks changes in solar activity that can influence space weather and satellite conditions near Earth.
lesson.simpler: The Sun has a repeating magnetic rhythm. When that rhythm gets stronger, more sunspots appear; when it weakens, fewer do.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Sunspots follow an 11-year cycle because the Sun's magnetic field is constantly building, twisting, and reversing.

learnImageCredit.creator: The original uploader was Ascánder at Spanish Wikibooks.

lesson.deeperDive: The solar dynamo is the engine behind the sunspot cycle. Hot plasma moving inside the Sun carries electric currents that generate magnetic fields, and the Sun's rotation stretches and organizes those fields over time. Eventually the field geometry becomes tangled enough that the polarity of the Sun's magnetic field flips, which marks the transition between solar cycles. The exact length and strength of each cycle vary, which is why some cycles produce many sunspots while others are relatively quiet. That variability can affect the frequency of flares, coronal mass ejections, and the overall space-weather environment around Earth.
lesson.funFact: Sunspots are not dark because they are cold in an absolute sense; they are just cooler than the surrounding surface, so they look dark by comparison.
references: Review articles on the solar dynamo

people: Heinrich Schwabe - first documented the cycle
George Ellery Hale - revealed sunspot magnetism
Edward Maunder - tracked long-term cycle behavior
NOAA solar cycle forecasters - modern cycle monitoring
places: The solar photosphere - where sunspots appear
NOAA space weather centers - cycle monitoring
Earth orbit - where solar activity can affect satellites
years: 2020s - Solar Cycle 25 is tracked in detail
